# Coursework1_221117152_Time-Series

## Project description
Time series analysis and model for web search history on "exercise" using R and Meta Prophet

<h1 class="title toc-ignore">Coursework1_221117152</h1>

<p>As someone that runs and exercises a lot in my free time, I wanted to
understand people’s interest in exercising and whether it has improved
over the years. My time series is taken from Google Trends and gives
information on the indexed volume of web search history for the term
“Exercise” in GB from 2011 to 2025. First, plot the time series and
model it using simple linear regression.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>gb_exercise &lt;- read.csv(&quot;C:/Users/rache/OneDrive/Desktop/Year 4/Sem 2 - Time series/Coursework1_221117152/GB_exercise_searchHistory.csv&quot;)
gb_exercise &lt;- ts(gb_exercise$exercise, start = c(2011, 1), frequency = 12)

exercise_lm &lt;- lm(gb_exercise ~ time(gb_exercise))
plot(gb_exercise, main = &quot;Web search for exercise in GB&quot;, type = &quot;l&quot;)
abline(exercise_lm, col = &quot;red&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<pre class="r"><code>plot(residuals(exercise_lm), main = &quot;Residuals of linear model&quot;, type = &quot;l&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" />
By plotting the search history of exercise, there are two key takeaways.
The search volume has a seasonal pattern and search volume spiked during
the covid-19 pandemic. Search volume is much higher at the start of the
year and dips at the end of the year. The linear regression model is
therefore not a good fit as it cannot capture any seasonal patterns.
Next, we model the time series with the decompose function and explore
the trend, seasonality, and residual components.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>exercise_decom &lt;- decompose(gb_exercise, type = &quot;additive&quot;)</code></pre>
<p>Trend component shows an gradual increase in search history for
exercise from 2011 to 2020, followed by a spike in 2020 and 2021. Search
history then drops back to pre-2020 levels in 2022 to 2025. The trend
generally makes sense. The gradual increase in search history from 2011
to 2020 can be explained by the increase availability of mobile phones
and technology. As mobile phones are more accessible, people are able to
use technology in their every day lives for non-work purposes. The huge
spike corresponds to the covid-19 pandemic, where people are stuck at
home, hence have more time to consider exercise. The reduction is search
history from 2022 onwards corresponds to the end to the covid-19
pandemic, where people are no longer working remotely and have less time
to exercise.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>plot(exercise_decom$trend, main = &quot;Trend from the decomposed time series&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<p>Seasonality component shows a clear decreasing trend in search
history for exercise over the year. We can see steep reductions in the
search history from April to August and from November to December. We
expect the search history to be the higest in Janurary as most people
try to loose weight after the holiday season and make new year
resolutions during Janurary.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>plot(exercise_decom$figure, main = &quot;Seasonality from the decomposed time series&quot;, type = &quot;l&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<p>Resdiual component shows huge residuals in 2020 and 2021. This is
expected as the covid-19 pandemic happened during this period and models
cannot capture these “unexpected circumstances” unless we add an
additional model specific for the pandemic. If we ignore the residuals
from 2020 to 2021, the residuals look random.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>plot(exercise_decom$random, main = &quot;Resdiuals from the decomposed time series&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" />
We can now test the properties of the residuals. Adf test is used to
test if time series is stationary. Ljung-Box test is used to test if
time series is white noise. The p-value for adf test is less than 5%, so
residuals are stationary. However, the p-value for Ljung-Box test is
less than 5%, indicating the residuals are not white noise.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>#testing properties of residuals
exercise_decom_resid &lt;- zoo::na.trim(exercise_decom$random)
#adf test
#H0: Time series is not stationary
tseries::adf.test(exercise_decom_resid) #p-value = 0.01 &lt; 0.05</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Registered S3 method overwritten by &#39;quantmod&#39;:
##   method            from
##   as.zoo.data.frame zoo</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Warning in tseries::adf.test(exercise_decom_resid): p-value smaller than
## printed p-value</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 
##  Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
## 
## data:  exercise_decom_resid
## Dickey-Fuller = -8.2438, Lag order = 5, p-value = 0.01
## alternative hypothesis: stationary</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>#Ljung box test
#H0: Time series is not white noise
Box.test(exercise_decom_resid, type = &quot;Ljung-Box&quot;)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 
##  Box-Ljung test
## 
## data:  exercise_decom_resid
## X-squared = 45.014, df = 1, p-value = 1.957e-11</code></pre>
<p>I took a subset of the time series so we model only post covid data.
However, there is still auto-correlation in the residuals.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>exercise_subset &lt;- window(gb_exercise, start = c(2012,1))
exercise_subset_decom &lt;- decompose(exercise_subset, type = &quot;additive&quot;)
plot(exercise_subset_decom)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<pre class="r"><code>plot(exercise_subset_decom$random, main = &quot;Residuals for post-covid model&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<pre class="r"><code>exercise_subset_resid &lt;- zoo::na.trim(exercise_decom$random)
tseries::adf.test(exercise_subset_resid)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Warning in tseries::adf.test(exercise_subset_resid): p-value smaller than
## printed p-value</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 
##  Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
## 
## data:  exercise_subset_resid
## Dickey-Fuller = -8.2438, Lag order = 5, p-value = 0.01
## alternative hypothesis: stationary</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>Box.test(exercise_subset_resid, type = &quot;Ljung-Box&quot;)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 
##  Box-Ljung test
## 
## data:  exercise_subset_resid
## X-squared = 45.014, df = 1, p-value = 1.957e-11</code></pre>
<p>Run prophet to model time series and use it to predict search history
volume for the next 5 years. Looks like the prophet prediction shows a
reduction in search volume for 2026.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>library(prophet)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Warning: package &#39;prophet&#39; was built under R version 4.5.2</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Loading required package: Rcpp</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Warning: package &#39;Rcpp&#39; was built under R version 4.5.2</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Loading required package: rlang</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Warning: package &#39;rlang&#39; was built under R version 4.5.2</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>library(zoo)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Warning: package &#39;zoo&#39; was built under R version 4.5.2</code></pre>
<pre><code>## 
## Attaching package: &#39;zoo&#39;</code></pre>
<pre><code>## The following objects are masked from &#39;package:base&#39;:
## 
##     as.Date, as.Date.numeric</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>ds &lt;- as.yearmon(time(gb_exercise))
y &lt;- gb_exercise
exercise_df &lt;- data.frame(ds, y)
m &lt;- prophet(exercise_df)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Disabling weekly seasonality. Run prophet with weekly.seasonality=TRUE to override this.</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>f &lt;- make_future_dataframe(m, periods = 60, freq = &quot;month&quot;)
p &lt;- predict(m, f)
plot(m, p, main = &quot;Modelled time series using Prophet&quot;,
             xlab = &quot;Time&quot;, ylab = &quot;Search volume&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<p>To remove the covid effect from the model, I applied the prophet
method to pre-covid and post-covid time frames and see what trends are
obtained.</p>
<p>The pre-covid data predicts that search volume will display a
decreasing trend over time. The decrease is driven by a reduction in
search volume from 2017 to 2019. Intuitively, I would expect search
volume to increase given increase awareness of the importance of
exercise, but from data perspective, search volume is predicted to
decrease.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>exercise_subset &lt;- window(gb_exercise, end = c(2019,12))
ds &lt;- as.yearmon(time(exercise_subset))
y &lt;- exercise_subset
exercise_df &lt;- data.frame(ds, y)
m &lt;- prophet(exercise_df)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Disabling weekly seasonality. Run prophet with weekly.seasonality=TRUE to override this.</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>f &lt;- make_future_dataframe(m, periods = 132, freq = &quot;month&quot;)
p &lt;- predict(m, f)
plot(m, p, main = &quot;Time series subset using Prophet&quot;,
             xlab = &quot;Time&quot;, ylab = &quot;Search volume&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<p>Repeat the prophet method on post covid data. Here we see that a
increased search volume over the next 5 years. The increasing trend is
driven by the increase in search volume from 2023 to 2025. This makes
more intuitive sense as I expect people to have an increase awareness in
the importance of exercise over time.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>exercise_subset &lt;- window(gb_exercise, start = c(2022,1))
ds &lt;- as.yearmon(time(exercise_subset))
y &lt;- exercise_subset
exercise_df &lt;- data.frame(ds, y)
m &lt;- prophet(exercise_df)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Disabling weekly seasonality. Run prophet with weekly.seasonality=TRUE to override this.</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>f &lt;- make_future_dataframe(m, periods = 60, freq = &quot;month&quot;)
p &lt;- predict(m, f)
plot(m, p, main = &quot;Time series subset using Prophet&quot;,
             xlab = &quot;Time&quot;, ylab = &quot;Search volume&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>
<p>Apply an alternative method of modelling to the post covid data using
ARIMA.</p>
<pre class="r"><code>library(forecast)</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Warning: package &#39;forecast&#39; was built under R version 4.5.2</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>exercise_arima &lt;- auto.arima(exercise_subset)
exercise_arima</code></pre>
<pre><code>## Series: exercise_subset 
## ARIMA(0,1,1)(1,1,0)[12] 
## 
## Coefficients:
##           ma1     sar1
##       -0.6627  -0.5101
## s.e.   0.1403   0.1538
## 
## sigma^2 = 6.283:  log likelihood = -82.89
## AIC=171.79   AICc=172.56   BIC=176.45</code></pre>
<pre class="r"><code>forecast_arima &lt;- forecast::forecast(exercise_arima, h = 60)
time_arima &lt;- seq(from = 2026, by = 1/12, length.out = 60)
plot(forecast_arima, main = &quot;ARIMA forecast&quot;)</code></pre>
<p><img role="img" aria-label src="data:image/png;base64," alt width="672" /></p>




</div>
